content:
# Deuteronomy 3 — Disagreement Log
**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 3 (29 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Total disagreements logged:** 17

---

## V.1 — "by the way of Bashan" vs. "the road to Bashan"

- **EMET:** "went up the way of the Bashan"
- **LOGOS:** "went up by the way of Bashan"
- **PNEUMA:** "went up the road to Bashan"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "went up by the way of Bashan" (LOGOS) — the Hebrew *derekh ha-Bashan* with the preposition "by" is natural English.

---

## V.2 — "I have given him into your hand" vs. "I have handed him over"

- **EMET:** "into your hand I have given him"
- **LOGOS:** "I have given him...and all his people and his land into your hand"
- **PNEUMA:** "I have handed him over to you along with all his forces and his land"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "I have given him and all his people and his land into your hand" (LOGOS) — the formula *natatti beyad'kha* is standard; "forces" (PNEUMA) is an interpretive substitution for "people."

---

## V.6 — "devoted to destruction" vs. "completely destroyed"

- **EMET:** "devoted to destruction as we did to Sichon...devoting to destruction every city"
- **LOGOS:** "devoted them to destruction...every city devoted to destruction"
- **PNEUMA:** "completely destroyed them...every city, men, women, and children"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "devoted them to destruction...every city devoted to destruction" (LOGOS). The *cherem* theological term must be preserved. PNEUMA's "completely destroyed" loses this covenantal/cultic valence.

---

## V.9 — "Sidonians" vs. "Tsidoni'im"

- **EMET:** "Tsidoni'im"
- **LOGOS:** "Sidonians"
- **PNEUMA:** "Sidonians"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Sidonians" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — conventional English form.

---

## V.11 — "his bed was a bed of iron" vs. "His bed was made of iron"

- **EMET:** "his bedstead [was] a bedstead of iron"
- **LOGOS:** "his bed was a bed of iron"
- **PNEUMA:** "His bed was made of iron"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "his bed was a bed of iron" (LOGOS). The emphatic Hebrew double-noun construction (*arso arset barzel*) is best preserved by EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the structural emphasis.

---

## V.11 — "sons of Ammon" vs. "children of Ammon" vs. "Ammonites"

- **EMET:** "Rabbah of the sons of Ammon"
- **LOGOS:** "Rabbah of the children of Ammon"
- **PNEUMA:** "Rabbah of the Ammonites"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Rabbah of the sons of Ammon" — project convention for *bney* genealogical formula.

---

## V.13 — "called the land of the Rephaim" formulation

- **EMET:** "all that Bashan is called 'the land of the Refa'im'"
- **LOGOS:** "That is called the land of the Rephaim"
- **PNEUMA:** "(The whole region of Argob in Bashan used to be called the land of the Rephaim)"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "All that Bashan is called the land of the Rephaim" — preserves the *kol ha-Bashan* scope while keeping the parenthetical character.

---

## V.14 — "Havvoth-jair" transliteration

- **EMET:** "Chavvot Ya'ir"
- **LOGOS:** "Havvoth-jair"
- **PNEUMA:** "Havvoth-jair"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Havvoth-jair" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — the standard English transliteration.

---

## V.17 — "Chinnereth" vs. "Kinneret" vs. "Kinnereth"

- **EMET:** "Kinneret"
- **LOGOS:** "Chinnereth"
- **PNEUMA:** "Kinnereth"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Chinnereth" (LOGOS) — the conventional English transliteration.

---

## V.18 — "men of valor" vs. "able men" vs. "fighting men"

- **EMET:** "all the able men"
- **LOGOS:** "all the men of valor"
- **PNEUMA:** "All your fighting men"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "all the men of valor" (LOGOS). The Hebrew *kol bney chayil* — "sons of valor" — is an honorific military term. "Valor" preserves its nuance; "able men" and "fighting men" are more prosaic.

---

## V.22 — emphasis construction

- **EMET:** "the LORD your God — he is fighting for you"
- **LOGOS:** "it is the LORD your God who fights for you"
- **PNEUMA:** "the LORD your God himself will fight for you"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "it is the LORD your God who fights for you" (LOGOS). The Hebrew *YHWH Elohekhem hu ha-nilcham lakem* carries the emphatic pronoun *hu* (he). LOGOS's "it is...who" construction reflects this most clearly in English.

---

## V.23 — "pleaded" vs. "entreated"

- **EMET:** "I entreated the LORD at that time"
- **LOGOS:** "I pleaded with the LORD at that time"
- **PNEUMA:** "I pleaded with the LORD"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "I pleaded with the LORD at that time" (LOGOS). "Pleaded" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is more natural English for *va-etchannan* (the Hithpa'el of *chanan* — to implore grace).

---

## V.24 — "O Lord GOD" vs. "O Sovereign LORD"

- **EMET:** "O Lord GOD"
- **LOGOS:** "O Lord GOD"
- **PNEUMA:** "O Sovereign LORD"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "O Lord GOD" (EMET/LOGOS). This is the conventional rendering of *Adonai YHWH*. "Sovereign" (PNEUMA) is interpretive — it captures one dimension of *Adonai* but imposes a specific semantic frame.

---

## V.24 — "strong hand" vs. "mighty hand" vs. "powerful hand"

- **EMET:** "your strong hand"
- **LOGOS:** "your mighty hand"
- **PNEUMA:** "your powerful hand"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "your strong hand" (EMET). The Hebrew *chazaqah* = strength, firmness. "Strong" is the most direct rendering.

---

## V.25 — "this good hill country" vs. "that beautiful hill country"

- **EMET:** "this good hill-country and the Levanon"
- **LOGOS:** "this good hill country and the Lebanon"
- **PNEUMA:** "that beautiful hill country and Lebanon"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "this good hill country and Lebanon" (LOGOS). The Hebrew *ha-har ha-tov ha-zeh* uses the proximate demonstrative *zeh* (this), not the distal *hu*. "Beautiful" (PNEUMA) and "that" (PNEUMA) both depart from the MT.

---

## V.26 — "Enough!" formulation

- **EMET:** "Enough for you — do not add to speak to me further of this matter"
- **LOGOS:** "Enough of that! Do not speak to me again about this matter"
- **PNEUMA:** "Enough! Do not speak to me again about this matter"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Enough! Do not speak to me again about this matter." Single exclamation is most effective; LOGOS's "of that" is slightly redundant. EMET's fuller expansion of *rav lakh* is technically accurate but verbose.

---

## V.28 — "charge Joshua" vs. "command" vs. "commission"

- **EMET:** "command Yehoshua"
- **LOGOS:** "charge Joshua"
- **PNEUMA:** "commission Joshua"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "charge Joshua" (LOGOS). The Hebrew *tsav* means to command/charge; in the context of formal succession the solemn "charge" is preferred over the more bureaucratic "commission" (PNEUMA) or the generic "command" (EMET).
